extra.reasoning: The invariant opted_out => not sent is inductive and holds in every reachable state. Justification by case analysis on the only writers of the two variables: (1) sent is set to true ONLY by [t2] send_attempt, whose guard is `not opted_out`; thus any step that sets sent:=true necessarily occurs in a state where opted_out is false, so the implication is vacuously satisfied at that step. (2) opted_out is set to true ONLY by [t1] optout, which in the same atomic update also sets sent:=false; so when opted_out becomes true, sent is simultaneously false. (3) While opted_out is already true, consider every event: [t1] optout keeps opted_out=true and sets sent:=false (holds); [t2] send_attempt is disabled because its guard `not opted_out` is false, so it cannot set sent:=true; [t3] send_attempt fires and sets sent:=false (holds); [t4] tick sets sent:=false (holds). Hence once opted_out is true, no transition can make sent true, and every applicable transition forces sent=false. The initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false) satisfies the invariant vacuously. By induction the invariant holds on all reachable states, well within the 6-event bound, so globally(opted_out implies not sent) is satisfied. No counterexample exists.
